name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
مَا كَانَ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَن يَعْمُرُوا۟ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ شَـٰهِدِينَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِم بِٱلْكُفْرِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ وَفِى ٱلنَّارِ هُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
The polytheists may not maintain Allah’s mosques while they are witness to their own unfaith. Their works have failed, and they shall remain in the Fire [forever]. (17)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا يَعْمُرُ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَأَقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَى ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَلَمْ يَخْشَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۖ فَعَسَىٰٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَن يَكُونُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (١٨)
```

```english
Only those shall maintain Allah’s mosques who believe in Allah and the Last Day, and maintain the prayer, and give the zakat, and fear no one except Allah. They, hopefully, will be among the guided. (18)
```

```arabic
۞ أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقَايَةَ ٱلْحَآجِّ وَعِمَارَةَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ كَمَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَجَـٰهَدَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ لَا يَسْتَوُۥنَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٩)
```

```english
Do you regard the providing of water to Hajj pilgrims and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque as similar [in worth] to someone who has faith in Allah and [believes in] the Last Day and wages jihad in the way of Allah? They are not equal with Allah, and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (19)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (٢٠)
```

```english
Those who have believed and migrated, and waged jihad in the way of Allah with their possessions and persons have a greater rank near Allah, and it is they who are the triumphant. (20)
```

```arabic
يُبَشِّرُهُمْ رَبُّهُم بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ وَرِضْوَٰنٍ وَجَنَّـٰتٍ لَّهُمْ فِيهَا نَعِيمٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٢١)
```

```english
Their Lord gives them the good news of His mercy and [His] pleasure, and for them there will be gardens with lasting bliss, (21)
```

```arabic
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (٢٢)
```

```english
to remain in them forever. With Allah indeed is a great reward. (22)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَكُمْ وَإِخْوَٰنَكُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ إِنِ ٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ ٱلْكُفْرَ عَلَى ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُم مِّنكُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not befriend your fathers and brothers if they prefer faithlessness to faith. Those of you who befriend them—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (23)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِن كَانَ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَآؤُكُمْ وَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُكُمْ وَعَشِيرَتُكُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلٌ ٱقْتَرَفْتُمُوهَا وَتِجَـٰرَةٌ تَخْشَوْنَ كَسَادَهَا وَمَسَـٰكِنُ تَرْضَوْنَهَآ أَحَبَّ إِلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَجِهَادٍ فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٢٤)
```

```english
Say, ‘If your fathers and your sons, your brethren, your spouses, and your kinsfolk, the possessions that you have acquired, the business you fear may suffer, and the dwellings you are fond of, are dearer to you than Allah and His Apostle and to waging jihad in His way, then wait until Allah issues His edict, and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (24)
```

## (Commentary)

These verses clarify that deeds are only living and pleasing to Allah when they emanate from the reality of faith (الإيمان) — *the firm belief and inner conviction* — in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day; otherwise, they are merely frustrated (حبط) — *rendered null and void of any spiritual reward* — and do not lead their doer to felicity (سعادة). Furthermore, among the necessary implications of faith in its true reality is the restriction of guardianship (الولاية), love, and affection to Allah and His Messenger.

These verses exhibit a clear connection and cohesion among themselves. As for their connection to the preceding verses, it is not as obvious, and what some commentators have mentioned regarding the mode of their connection to what came before is not free from affectation (تكلف).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*ما كانَ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَنْ يَعْمُرُوا مَساجِدَ اللهِ شاهِدِينَ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ بِالْكُفْرِ*» — **It is not for the polytheists to maintain Allah’s mosques, while witnessing to their own unfaith (9:17).** Maintenance (العمارة) is the opposite of ruin. It is said: "He maintained (عمر) the land" when he built upon it, and "He maintained the house" when he repaired what was on the verge of decay. The term *ta'mir* (التعمير) carries this meaning, and from it comes the word *'umr* (العمر) — *lifespan* — because it is the maintenance of the body by the soul. The *'umrah* (العمرة) — *the lesser pilgrimage* — is so named because it involves visiting and thus maintaining the Sacred House.

The mosque (المسجد) is a noun of place meaning the location associated with prostration (السجدة), such as the building erected for Allah, the Exalted, to be prostrated within. It also refers to the organs of prostration (أعضاء السجدة) which have a specific connection to the act of prostrating: the forehead, the two palms, the two knees, and the tips of the two big toes.

His saying: «*ما كانَ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ*» (It is not for the polytheists) serves to negate both right and ownership, as the letter *lam* (لـ) denotes ownership and right. The negation of the present state of "being" (الكون) implies that no prior cause has been realized on their part that would grant them this right—namely, the right to maintain the mosques of Allah, repair what has fallen into ruin, or visit them. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: «*ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى*» — **It is not for a prophet to take captives (8:67)** and His saying: «*وَما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَغُلَّ*» — **A prophet may not leave a debt unpaid (3:161).**

What is intended by maintenance in His saying: «*أَنْ يَعْمُرُوا*» (that they maintain) is the repair of what is on the verge of ruin in the structure and the restoration of what has decayed, rather than maintenance through visitation. This is because "the mosques of Allah" refers to the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) and every mosque belonging to Allah, yet the *'umrah* (visitation) is not performed except at the Sacred Mosque. While entering mosques for worship can be called maintenance or visitation, the customary expression used in the Quran for this is "entering" (الدخول).

Furthermore, His saying in the subsequent verse: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِّ وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ*» (Do you reckon the providing of water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque...) provides some support for the view that maintenance refers to the repair of the structure rather than the visitation of the Sacred House.

"The mosques of Allah" refers to the houses of worship built for Allah, but the context indicates that the specific intent is to negate the permissibility of the polytheists maintaining the Sacred Mosque. This is supported by the variant reading of those who read "the mosque of Allah" (مسجد الله) in the singular.

There is no harm in using the plural form when the primary objective is to clarify the ruling for a specific individual instance, because the underlying rationale (الملاك) is universal. The justification provided in the verse is not restricted specifically to the Sacred Mosque; rather, the speech means: It is not for them to maintain the Sacred Mosque because it is a mosque, and this is the status of all mosques.

His saying: «*شاهِدِينَ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ بِالْكُفْرِ*» (while witnessing to their own unfaith) — the "witnessing" intended here is its performance, which is confession (الاعتراف). This can be verbal, as in one who confesses to disbelief in words, or behavioral, as in one who worships idols and manifests his disbelief. All of this constitutes witnessing, and the underlying rationale is the same.

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: The polytheists have no right, nor is it permissible for them, to repair what has decayed of the Sacred Mosque—just like any other mosque of Allah—while they are confessing to disbelief through the evidence of their words or deeds.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*أُولئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ وَفِي النَّارِ هُمْ خالِدُونَ*» — **The deeds of such are null, and they shall abide in the Fire (9:17).** This serves as the justification (التعليل) for the ruling provided in His saying: "It is not for..." etc. For this reason, it was brought as a separate sentence (فصل) without a conjunction (وصل).

The first clause is intended to clarify the invalidity of the effect and its removal from their deeds. A deed is only performed as a means (التوسل) to a desired effect; since their deeds are frustrated (حابطة) and produce no effect, there is nothing that makes it permissible for them to perform them. Devotional acts, such as maintaining the mosques of Allah, are only intended for the sake of the effect one hopes for and desires—namely, felicity and Paradise. A frustrated deed never results in felicity or Paradise.

The second clause is intended to clarify the place where they will settle in the absence of felicity and Paradise, which is the Fire. It is as if it were said: Their devotional acts do not guide them to Paradise; rather, they are in the eternal Fire. These acts do not benefit them with felicity; rather, they are in everlasting wretchedness (الشقاوة).

The verse contains an indication of two subtle principles of legislation (أصول التشريع):

First: The legislation of permissibility (الجواز) — in its general sense encompassing the obligatory (الواجبات), the recommended (المستحبات), and the permissible (المباحات) — depends on an effect in the act that benefits the doer. Thus, there is no futile (لغو) act legislated in the religion. This is a principle supported by reason and is consistent with the law governing the universe: that no act exists except for a benefit returning to its agent.

Second: Permissibility in all its instances is preceded by a right (حق) established by Allah for the agent to perform the act without hindrance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*إِنَّما يَعْمُرُ مَساجِدَ اللهِ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ*» — **Only he shall maintain Allah’s mosques who believes in Allah and the Last Day... (9:18).** The context reveals that the restriction (الحصر) is of the type known as "restriction of isolation" (قصر الإفراد) — *a rhetorical device used to isolate one attribute to a single subject when it might be thought to apply to others*. It was imagined that both the polytheists and the believers had the right to maintain Allah's mosques, so the verse isolated and restricted this right to the believers. Consequently, the word "maintains" (يعمر) must be intended to establish the right and permissibility in the form of a statement, rather than being a mere report of fact, which is evident.

Allah, the Glorified, has stipulated for the establishment of the right of maintenance and its permissibility that the maintainer be characterized by faith in Allah and the Last Day. This is in contrast to what was negated for the polytheists. He did not suffice with faith in Allah alone because the polytheists acknowledge Him, the Exalted; rather, He coupled it with faith in the Last Day because the polytheists did not believe in it. By this, the right of maintenance and its permissibility are restricted to the people of the divine religion among the believers.

He did not suffice with that either, but appended to it His saying: «*وَأقامَ الصَّلاةَ وَآتَى الزَّكاةَ وَلَمْ يَخْشَ إِلَّا اللهَ*» (...and maintains the prayer and gives the zakat, and fears no one except Allah). This is because the context is one of clarifying who benefits from his deed and thus has the right to perform it. Whoever abandons the branches (الفروع) — *the practical laws and rituals* — legislated in the religion, especially the two pillars: prayer (الصلاة) and almsgiving (الزكاة), is a disbeliever in the signs of Allah. Mere faith in Allah and the Last Day does not benefit him, even if he is a Muslim, provided he does not deny them with his tongue; if he were to deny them with his tongue as well, he would be a disbeliever and not a Muslim.

He specifically mentioned prayer and almsgiving among them because they are the two pillars that are indispensable under any circumstances.

From what we have mentioned regarding the requirements of the context, it becomes clear that what is intended by His saying: «*وَلَمْ يَخْشَ إِلَّا اللهَ*» (and fears no one except Allah) is religious fear (الخشية الدينية), which is worship (العبادة), rather than instinctive fear (الخشية الغريزية) from which no one is safe except the near ones (المقربون) among the friends of Allah, such as the prophets. The Exalted said: «*الَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسالاتِ اللهِ وَيَخْشَوْنَهُ وَلا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللهَ*» — **...those who deliver the messages of Allah and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah (33:39).**

The reason for using "fear" as a metonym (التكنية) for worship is that the most well-known reasons for a human taking a deity for worship are fear of its wrath or hope for its mercy. Hope for mercy also returns, in a way, to the fear of its cessation, which is wrath. Thus, whoever worships Allah, the Glorified, or worships an idol, is driven to it either by fear of His wrath encompassing him or fear of the cessation of His blessing and mercy. Worship represents fear, and fear (الخشية) is an instance (مصداق) of it because it represents it, and there is a state of entailment (استلزام) between them. Therefore, it was used as a metonym for it. The meaning — and Allah knows best — is: "and he worships no one besides Allah among the deities."

His saying: «*فَعَسى أُولئِكَ أَنْ يَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُهْتَدِينَ*» — **...maybe such will be among the guided (9:18).** That is, those who believe in Allah and the Last Day and worship no one other than Allah, the Glorified, it is hoped for them that they will be among the guided. This hope (الرجاء) exists within themselves or within the addressees of the verse. As for Him, the Exalted, it is impossible for hope to exist in Him, as hope is only complete alongside ignorance of whether the hoped-for matter will be realized.

Guidance (الاهتداء) was taken as something hoped for rather than something certain to occur, even though one who truly believes in Allah and the Last Day and confirms it with his devotional acts has indeed been guided in reality. This is because the occurrence of guidance once or several times does not necessitate that the doer is "among the guided" (المهتدين) — *those in whom the quality of being guided has become a stable and inseparable attribute*. Engaging in an act that occurs once or several times is different from being characterized by an inseparable attribute (الصفة اللازمة). For those people, the occurrence of guidance is certain, but the attainment of the attribute of "the guided" is hoped for, not certain.

It is concluded from the verse that the maintenance of mosques is neither a right nor permissible for a non-Muslim. As for the polytheists, it is due to their lack of faith in Allah and the Last Day. As for the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), it is because the Quran does not consider their faith in Allah to be true faith. The Exalted said: «*إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلاً أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ حَقًّا*» — **Those who disbelieve in Allah and His apostles and seek to separate Allah from His apostles, and say, ‘We believe in some and disbelieve in some’ and seek to take a way in between—it is they who are truly faithless (4:150-151).** He also said in verse 29 of this Surah: «*قاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَلا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَلا يُحَرِّمُونَ ما حَرَّمَ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَلا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ الْحَقِّ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ*» — **Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day, and who do not forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, and who do not practise the true religion, from among those who were given the Book... (9:29).**

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِّ وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ كَمَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَجاهَدَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ*» — **Do you reckon the providing of water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque to be like [the person] who has faith in Allah and the Last Day and wages jihad in the way of Allah? (9:19).** *Siqayah* (السقاية) is a verbal noun (مصدر), as in "he gave water (سقى), he gives water (يسقي), providing water (سقاية)."

*Siqayah* also refers to the place where water is given, and the vessel with which water is given. The Exalted said: «*جَعَلَ السِّقايَةَ فِي رَحْلِ أَخِيهِ*» — **...he put the drinking-cup into his brother’s saddlebag (12:70).** It has been narrated in the traditions that providing water to the pilgrims was one of the magnificent offices and noble deeds boasted of in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). The *siqayah* consisted of basins made of leather during the time of Qusayy ibn Kilab, one of the ancestors of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, placed in the courtyard of the Kaaba. Fresh water from wells was brought to them on camels to provide drink for the pilgrims. Upon his death, Qusayy entrusted the matter of *siqayah* to his son Abd Manaf, and it remained with his descendants until it was inherited by al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib.

The *siqayah* of al-Abbas is the place where water was provided during the Age of Ignorance and Islam; it is located to the south of Zamzam, with forty cubits between them. A structure was built over it, which is known today as the *Siqayah* of al-Abbas.

In any case, what is intended by *siqayah* in the verse is its verbal noun meaning, which is the act of providing water. This is supported by its being contrasted in the verse with the "maintenance of the Sacred Mosque," where maintenance certainly carries the verbal noun meaning of an occupation or task.

In the verse, the providing of water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of the Sacred Mosque are contrasted with "one who believes in Allah and the Last Day and strives in the way of Allah." There is no meaning in claiming equality—or negating it—between a human being and an action such as providing water or maintenance. Therefore, the comparison and equality must be either between one action and another, or between a person who performs an action and another person who performs an action.

For this reason, the commentators were compelled to say that the implied meaning of the speech is: "Do you reckon the *people* of providing water to the pilgrims and the *people* of maintaining the Sacred Mosque to be like one who believes in Allah and the Last Day," so that the context remains sound.

More necessary than that is to examine the constraints (القيود) of the speech employed in the noble verse. On one side, providing water to the pilgrims and maintaining the Sacred Mosque are taken alone without any additional constraint. On the other side, faith in Allah and the Last Day and striving (الجهاد) in the way of Allah are taken—or if you prefer, striving in the way of Allah with the consideration of faith alongside it.

This indicates that what is intended is: providing water and maintenance devoid of faith. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: «*وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ*» (and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people), assuming this is an allusion (تعريض) to the people of providing water and maintenance, rather than an allusion to those who equate the two, as is immediately suggested by the context.

This reveals, firstly, that those who were equating this and that were equating a pre-Islamic deed devoid of faith in Allah and the Last Day—such as providing water and maintenance without faith—with a religious deed stemming from faith in Allah and the Last Day—such as striving in the way of Allah out of faith. That is, they were equating the "body" of a deed that has no life with a living, good, and beneficial deed; thus, Allah denounced them for it.

Secondly: These who were equating were among the believers, equating a deed performed without faith—whether it issued from them before their faith or from a polytheist other than them—with a deed that issued from a believer in Allah out of pure faith during his state of belief. This is attested to by the context of denunciation and the clarification of ranks (الدرجات) in the verses.

Rather, the mention of providing water and maintenance themselves, without mentioning their performers, suggests—or even indicates—that their performers were among the people of faith at the time of the comparison. Thus, they were not mentioned to preserve their dignity, as they were believers at the time of the address, and to protect them from being explicitly named as "wrongdoers" (ظالمين) in light of the apparent allusion at the end of the verse.

Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted, in the following verse, in the context of clarifying the reward of these who strive in the way of Allah out of faith: «*الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَهاجَرُوا وَجاهَدُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ*» (Those who have faith and migrate and wage jihad in the way of Allah...) indicates that both parties of the comparison in His saying: "Do you reckon the providing of water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque to be like [the person] who has faith..." were from the people of Mecca. It indicates that the people of one side—those who believed and strove—were among those who submitted and migrated (هاجر), while the people of the other side submitted but did not migrate. This is the reason for His mentioning faith and striving on one side first, and then adding migration (الهجرة) to it when it was repeated a second time. He mentioned providing water and maintenance on the other side and added nothing to it, neither the first time nor the second. These constraints are indeed rhetorical (بلاغية) in His decisive speech.

All of this supports what has been narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of the verse: that the verses were revealed concerning al-Abbas, Shaybah, and Ali (peace be upon him) when they boasted to one another. Al-Abbas mentioned providing water to the pilgrims, Shaybah mentioned the maintenance of the Sacred Mosque, and Ali mentioned faith and striving in the way of Allah. Thus, the verses were revealed, and the narration will follow in the "Discussion of Traditions" related to the verses.

In any case, the verse and those following it clarify that weight and value (القيمة) only belong to a deed when it is alive by the entry of the spirit of faith into it. As for the empty body that has no spirit or life, it has no weight in the balance of religion and no value in the market of realities. It is not for the believers to consider the mere structures of deeds and make them the criteria for merit and the means of proximity to Him, the Exalted, except after considering their life through faith and sincerity (الخلوص).

From this perspective, the verse: "Do you reckon the providing of water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque..." and the verses following it are connected to the two preceding verses: "It is not for the polytheists to maintain Allah’s mosques, while witnessing to their own unfaith..." until the end of the two verses.

By all of this, it becomes clear, firstly, that His saying: «*وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ*» (and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people) is a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية) clarifying the reason for the denunciation of their judgment of equality in His saying: "Do you reckon the providing of water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque to be like [the person] who has faith..." etc.

Secondly: What is intended by wrongdoing (الظلم) is the state of polytheism they were in while providing water and maintaining, not their judgment of equality between providing water and maintenance and striving out of faith.

Thirdly: What is intended is the negation that the deed will benefit them and guide them to felicity, which consists of the greatness of rank, success (الفوز), mercy, pleasure (الرضوان), and the eternal Paradise.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَهاجَرُوا وَجاهَدُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ*» — **Those who have faith and migrate and wage jihad in the way of Allah with their possessions and persons... (9:20)** until the end of the verse, is a clarification of the true judgment that is with Allah regarding the matter after the negation of equality. It is that the one who believed, migrated, and strove in the way of Allah to the best of his ability by sacrificing what he possessed of wealth and self, is greater in rank (أعظم درجة) with Allah. It was expressed in the plural form — "those who have faith" etc. — to indicate that the criterion for the distinction is the attribute (الوصف) rather than the person.

What has preceded regarding the indication of the speech that deeds without faith in Allah have no merit and no rank for their doer with Allah, serves as a context (قرينة) that the comparison indicated by the elative (أفعل التفضيل) in His saying: "greater in rank" (أعظم درجة) etc., does not mean that there is a shared participation in ranks between the two groups, except that the rank of the one who strove out of faith is greater than the one who provided water and maintained.

Rather, the intent is to clarify that the relationship between them is like the relationship of the superior to one who has no merit at all, similar to a comparison made between "more" and "less." Such a comparison requires the existence of a middle point between them to which they are compared; thus, there are three things: a middle thing taken as a standard measure, another that is more than it, and another that is less than it. When the "more" is compared to the "less," the "more" is being compared to that which has no "muchness" in it at all.

Thus, His saying: «*أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً عِنْدَ اللهِ*» (greater in rank with Allah) means in comparison to those who have no rank at all. This is a type of metonymy (كناية) indicating that there is no real relationship between the two groups, because one of them has a lofty standing in that which the other has no standing in at all.

This is also indicated by His saying: «*وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ*» — **...it is they who are the triumphant (9:20)**, which indicates the restriction of triumph (الفوز) to them and its establishment for them in a stable manner.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*يُبَشِّرُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنْهُ وَرِضْوانٍ وَجَنَّاتٍ*» — **Their Lord gives them the good news of His mercy and pleasure, and gardens... (9:21)** until the end of the two verses. The apparent context is that what He enumerates of merit for them is a clarification and detail of what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding their triumph, presented in the language of glad tidings (التبشير).

The meaning is: "Their Lord gives them" — i.e., these believers — "the good news of His mercy" — a great mercy whose extent cannot be measured — "and pleasure" — likewise — "and gardens for them wherein" — in those gardens — "is an enduring bliss" (نعيم مقيم) that neither ceases nor ends, while they are "abiding therein forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً), their eternity not being interrupted by any term or period.

Then, since the context is one of wonder and the perception of improbability (الاستبعاد) because it is a glad tiding of a great matter not familiar among the types of bliss we witness in this world, He removed the improbability by His saying: «*إِنَّ اللهَ عِنْدَهُ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ*» — **...Indeed with Allah is a great reward (9:22).**

The explanation of the meaning of His mercy and His pleasure (الرضوان) will come in a suitable place hereafter, and some discussion regarding them has already preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا آباءَكُمْ وَإِخْوانَكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ*» — **O you who have faith! Do not befriend your fathers and brothers... (9:23)** until the end of the verse. It forbids taking the disbelievers as guardians (أولياء), even if they are fathers and brothers, for the criterion is universal. The following verse forbids taking everyone as such, but the literal wording of the verse is a prohibition against taking fathers and brothers as guardians if they prefer (استحبوا) disbelief and favor it over faith.

He mentioned fathers and brothers specifically, rather than children and spouses—despite both groups, especially children, being beloved to them like fathers and brothers—because guardianship (التولي) allows the guardian to interfere in the affairs of the one guarded and to manage some aspects of his life. This is the danger that necessitates the prohibition of taking disbelievers as guardians, so that they do not interfere in their internal affairs, nor take hold of their hearts, nor cause the believers to refrain or recoil from undertaking what would displease or harm them. It is well known that such a negative effect is not expected from women and offspring except through an intermediary. Therefore, the prohibition of guardianship was specified for fathers and brothers, as they are the ones whose influence over the hearts of the believers and management of their affairs is feared.

The prohibition against taking disbelievers as guardians has appeared in several places in His speech, some of which preceded in Surah al-Ma'idah, Al 'Imran, al-Nisa', and al-A'raf. In them are severe warnings and grave threats, such as His saying, the Exalted: «*وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ*» — **...Any of you who takes them as friends is indeed one of them (5:51)**, and His saying: «*وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ اللهُ نَفْسَهُ*» — **...Allah warns you to beware of [disobeying] Him (3:28)**, and His saying: «*وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ فَلَيْسَ مِنَ اللهِ فِي شَيْءٍ*» — **...and whoever does that has nothing to do with Allah (3:28)**, and His saying: «*أَتُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَجْعَلُوا لِلَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطاناً مُبِيناً*» — **...Do you wish to give Allah a clear sanction against yourselves? (4:144).**

He warned them in the verse we are currently discussing by His saying: "And whoever among you takes them as friends, such are the wrongdoers," and He did not say: "And whoever among you takes them as friends, he is indeed one of them," because it is possible that some of them might imagine that he *is* of them because they are his fathers and brothers, so the threat would not produce a new effect in him that would drive him toward rejecting the guardianship.

In any case, His saying: «*وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ*» (And whoever among you takes them as friends, such are the wrongdoers), with the emphasis contained in the sentence—such as its being a nominal sentence, the entry of the *lam* on the predicate, and the pronoun of separation (ضمير الفصل)—indicates the realization of wrongdoing (الظلم) on their part and its stability within them. Allah has repeated in His speech that Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people, and He said in a similar verse in Surah al-Ma'idah: «*وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ*» — **...Any of you who takes them as friends is indeed one of them. Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people (5:51).** Thus, these people are deprived of divine guidance; none of their good deeds will benefit them in drawing felicity toward them or granting them triumph and success.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قُلْ إِنْ كانَ آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ وَإِخْوانُكُمْ*» — **Say, ‘If your fathers and your sons, your brethren... (9:24)** until the end of the verse. He turned from addressing them to addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, as an indication of turning away (الإعراض) from them, because it is sensed from their state that their hearts are inclined toward being occupied with that which renders the prohibition of taking their disbelieving fathers and brothers as guardians ineffective. It also indicates the presence of a motive in their souls to deviate from the command of Allah and His Messenger and from fighting the disbelievers in striving in the way of Allah, even if they are their fathers and brothers.

That which prevents them from this is the love (الحب) attached to other than Allah, His Messenger, and striving in His way. Allah, the Glorified, has enumerated the primary things to which the soul's love is attached among the ornaments of the life of this world: fathers, sons, brothers, spouses, and kin (العشيرة) — these are the ones brought together by the natural society through close or distant blood relationship or marriage — and the wealth they have acquired and amassed, the commerce whose dullness they fear, and the dwellings they find pleasing — these are the primary things upon which society is based in the second degree.

The Exalted mentioned that if they take the enemies of the religion as guardians and prefer the judgment of these matters over the love of Allah, His Messenger, and striving in His way, then let them wait (فليتربصوا) and expect until Allah brings about His command, and Allah does not guide the transgressing people (القوم الفاسقين).

It is evident that the condition — namely His saying: "If your fathers..." until His saying: "...in His way" — carries the meaning of saying: "If you do not desist from what He forbids you regarding taking the disbelieving fathers and brothers as guardians, by taking them as a cause that leads to the opposite of what He calls you to, and by your negligence in the matter of the purpose of the religion, which is striving in His way."

Thus, His saying in the response (الجزاء): «*فَتَرَبَّصُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ*» (then wait until Allah brings His command) must inevitably refer either to a command by which the breach and the failure of the purpose that occurred in the religion during their opposition is rectified, or to a punishment that comes to them for opposing the command of Allah and His Messenger and turning away from striving in His way.

However, His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: «*وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ*» (and Allah does not guide the transgressing people) suggests to them that they are then outside the garb of servitude, transgressing (فاسقون) against the command of Allah and His Messenger. Thus, they are far removed from Allah guiding them through their deeds and granting them success in aiding Allah and His Messenger, elevating the word of the religion, and erasing the traces of polytheism.

The end of the verse leads to the conclusion that what is intended by this "command" (الأمر) which Allah orders them to wait for until He brings it, is a command from Him, the Exalted, related to the victory of His religion and the elevation of His word. Thus, it corresponds to something like His saying, the Exalted, in Surah al-Ma'idah after verses forbidding the guardianship of disbelievers: «*يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَنْ يَرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ عَنْ دِينِهِ فَسوفَ يَأْتِي اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ يُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلا يَخافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لائِمٍ ذلِكَ فَضْلُ اللهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَاللهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ*» — **O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion, Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, dignified before the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer. That is Allah’s grace which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing (5:54).** This verse, with its constraints and specifics — as you see — corresponds to what the verse we are currently discussing indicates.

The meaning — and Allah knows best — is: If you take these as guardians and recoil from obeying Allah and His Messenger and from striving in His way, then wait until Allah brings His command and sends a people who love none but Allah, do not befriend His enemies, and undertake the victory of the religion and striving in the way of Allah in the best manner. For you are then transgressors (فاسقون); the religion does not benefit from you, nor does Allah guide any of your deeds to a true purpose or a desired felicity.

It has been said that what is intended by His saying: «*فَتَرَبَّصُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ*» (then wait until Allah brings His command) is an allusion to the Conquest of Mecca. This is not sound, for the address in the verse is to the believers among the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار), especially the Emigrants. These are the ones by whose hands Allah opened Mecca. It makes no sense for them to be addressed and told: "If your fathers and your sons... etc. are dearer to you than Allah and His Messenger and striving in His way, and you take them as guardians and recoil from obeying Allah and His Messenger and striving in His way, then wait until Allah opens Mecca by your hands and Allah does not guide the transgressing people," or "then wait until Allah opens Mecca and Allah will not guide you because of your transgression." Reflect on this.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِ*» (Do you reckon the providing of water to the pilgrims...): From the *Amali* of the Shaykh, with his chain of transmission from al-A'mash, from Salim ibn Abi al-Ja'd, reaching Abu Dharr — in the report of the Shura — regarding what Ali (peace be upon him) used as an argument against the people: He said to them regarding that: "Is there anyone among you besides me regarding whom this verse was revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِّ وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ ـ كَمَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَجاهَدَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ*» (Do you reckon the providing of water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque to be like [the person] who has faith in Allah and the Last Day and wages jihad in the way of Allah)?" They said: "No."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, he said: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "This verse was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him): «*الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَهاجَرُوا*» (Those who have faith and migrate...) until His saying: «*الْفائِزُونَ*» (the triumphant). Then He described what Ali (peace be upon him) has with Him, saying: «*يُبَشِّرُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنْهُ وَرِضْوانٍ وَجَنَّاتٍ لَهُمْ فِيها نَعِيمٌ مُقِيمٌ*» (Their Lord gives them the good news of His mercy and pleasure, and gardens for them wherein is an enduring bliss)."

In *al-Majma'*, al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani narrated with his chain of transmission from Abu Buraydah, from his father, who said: While Shaybah and al-Abbas were boasting to one another, Ali ibn Abi Talib passed by them and said: "What are you boasting about?"

Al-Abbas said: "I have been given a merit that no one else has been given: the providing of water to the pilgrims." Shaybah said: "I have been given the maintenance of the Sacred Mosque." Ali said: "And I say to you both: I have been given in my youth what you have not been given." They asked: "And what have you been given, O Ali?" He said: "I struck your snouts with the sword until you believed in Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, and His Messenger."

Al-Abbas stood up in anger, dragging his cloak, until he entered upon the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: "Do you not see how Ali has confronted me?" He said: "Call Ali for me." He was called, and the Prophet said: "What moved you, O Ali, to confront your uncle in such a way?" He replied: "O Messenger of Allah, I confronted him with the truth; if he wishes, let him be angry, and if he wishes, let him be pleased."

Then Gabriel (peace be upon him) descended and said: "O Muhammad, your Lord conveys peace to you and says: Recite to them: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِّ وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ ـ كَمَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ*» until His saying: «*إِنَّ اللهَ عِنْدَهُ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ*»."

In *Tafsir al-Tabari*, with his chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Ka'b al-Qurazi, who said: Talhah ibn Shaybah, al-Abbas, and Ali ibn Abi Talib boasted to one another. Talhah said: "I am the master of the House; its key is with me." Al-Abbas said: "I am the master of the *siqayah* and its overseer." Ali said: "I do not know what you two are saying; I prayed toward the Qiblah six months before the people, and I am the master of jihad." Then Allah revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِ*» the entire verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Faryabi narrated from Ibn Sirin, who said: Ali ibn Abi Talib came to Mecca and said to al-Abbas: "O uncle, will you not migrate? Will you not join the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله?" He replied: "I maintain the Sacred Mosque and serve as the gatekeeper of the House." Then Allah revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِ*» the verse. And he said to a group whom he named: "Will you not migrate? Will you not join the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله?" They said: "We will stay with our brothers, our kin, and our dwellings." Then Allah, the Exalted, revealed: «*قُلْ إِنْ كانَ آباؤُكُمْ*» the entire verse.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: Al-Abbas said when he was captured on the day of Badr: "If you have preceded us in Islam, migration, and jihad, we used to maintain the Sacred Mosque, provide water to the pilgrims, and ransom the captive (العاني)." Then Allah revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِ*» the verse, meaning that this was during polytheism, so what was done during polytheism is not accepted.

In the same source, Muslim, Abu Dawud, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Nu'man ibn Bashir, who said: I was at the pulpit of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله among a group of his companions. One of them said: "I do not care if I perform no deed for Allah after Islam except providing water to the pilgrims." Another said: "Rather, the maintenance of the Sacred Mosque." Another said: "Rather, striving in the way of Allah is better than what you have said."

Umar rebuked them and said: "Do not raise your voices at the pulpit of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله" — and that was on a Friday — "but when you have prayed the Friday prayer, I will enter upon the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and seek his verdict regarding that in which you differed." Then Allah revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِ*» until His saying: «*وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ*».

I say: The author of *al-Manar* said in his commentary after citing these last four narrations: "The reliable one among these narrations is the tradition of al-Nu'man due to the soundness of its chain and the agreement of its text with what the verses indicate—that their subject matter is the comparison of merit or equality between the service of the House and its gatekeeping—which are among the easy and pleasurable physical acts of righteousness—and faith, striving with wealth and self, and migration, which are the most difficult spiritual, physical, and financial acts of worship. The verses contain a rebuttal to all of them." End quote.

As for what he mentioned regarding the preponderance of al-Nu'man's narration over others due to the soundness of the chain, there are several points: First, the narration of al-Qurazi also agrees in its content with the narration of al-Hakim in *al-Mustadrak*, and he has authenticated it.

Second: Narrations of *tafsir*, if they are solitary reports (آحاد), have no probative force (حجية) except for what agrees with the contents of the verses to the extent that it agrees with them, as explained in the science of principles (أصول). This is because legal probative force revolves around the legal effects that follow; thus, it is restricted to legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية). As for what lies beyond them, such as narrations regarding stories and commentary devoid of legal rulings, there is no legal probative force in them.

As for rational probative force — meaning that of the reasonable people (العقلائية) — it has no place after the abundance of interpolation and fabrication in reports, especially reports of commentary and stories, except for what possesses definitive indicators (قرائن قطعية) that allow reliance upon the soundness of its text. Among these is the agreement of its text with the apparent meanings of the noble verses.

Therefore, what is important for the researcher of non-jurisprudential narrations is to investigate their agreement with the Book. If they agree with it, then that is the criterion for their consideration, even if they are also sound in chain, for that is merely an ornament with which they are adorned. If they do not agree, they have no value in the market of consideration.

As for abandoning the investigation of agreement with the Book and delving deeply into the investigation of the state of the chain — except for what serves as a means to obtain indicators — then judging the narration as considered due to the soundness of its chain, and then imposing what the text of the narration indicates upon the Book and making the Book follow it, as is the habit of many of them, this is something for which there is no path in terms of evidence.

As for what he mentioned regarding the preponderance of al-Nu'man's narration over others from the perspective of the text, explaining that the verses indicate that the subject of equality or comparison of merit was between the service of the House or its gatekeeping—which are among the easy and pleasurable physical acts of righteousness—and faith, migration, and striving—which are difficult spiritual and physical acts of righteousness—and that the verses contain a rebuttal to all of them. End quote.

There are several points here: First, what he mentioned regarding the meaning of the verses is common to all the narrations he cited:

As for the narration of Ibn Abbas, whose content is that the discussion regarding equality or comparison of merit occurred when al-Abbas was captured on the day of Badr between al-Abbas and the Muslims when they reproached him, it explicitly mentions the comparison between Islam, migration, and striving, and between providing water to the pilgrims, maintaining the Mosque, and ransoming the captive. There are other narrations to the same effect.

As for the narration of Ibn Sirin, which indicates that the dispute occurred between Ali and al-Abbas in Mecca when he invited him to migrate and join the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and he replied that he had the maintenance of the Sacred Mosque and the gatekeeping of the House—Ibn Mardawayh narrated this meaning from al-Sha'bi, in which it is stated: Al-Abbas said to Ali: "I am the uncle of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and you are his cousin; to me belongs the providing of water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of the Sacred Mosque." Then Allah revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِ*» the verse.

It was also narrated by Ibn Abi Shaybah, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh from Abdullah ibn Ubaydah, in which it is stated: Al-Abbas said to Ali: "Am I not in a state better than migration? Do I not provide water to the pilgrims and maintain the Sacred Mosque?" Then this verse was revealed.

In any case, what occurs in this narration is also a comparison between providing water and maintenance, and between migration and what follows from it of what joining the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله necessitates, such as striving and other noble religious deeds.

As for the narration of al-Qurazi and what is in its meaning—such as what al-Hakim narrated and authenticated, and what Abd al-Razzaq narrated from al-Hasan, who said: "It was revealed concerning Ali, al-Abbas, Uthman, and Shaybah who spoke regarding that," as well as the narration of al-Nu'man mentioned earlier—the fact that the dispute in them was regarding providing water, maintenance, faith, and striving is evident. If this is the case, what distinction is there in the narration of al-Nu'man ibn Bashir that would necessitate its exclusive agreement with the Book among all other narrations?

Second: His statement that the subject of comparison of merit is "easy and pleasurable acts of righteousness" such as providing water and gatekeeping, and "difficult acts of righteousness" such as faith, migration, and striving, does not agree with what the verses indicate. For they, as preceded, clearly indicate that the comparison was between them—between the "bodies" of deeds devoid of the spirit of faith, which are not acts of righteousness at that time—and deeds made alive by the entry of the spirit of faith into them, such as migration and striving out of faith in Allah and the Last Day.

The verses indicate that they were equating or preferring things other than acts of righteousness—such as providing water and maintenance without faith—over acts of righteousness—such as striving out of faith and migration, and migration out of faith. So where is what he mentioned regarding "easy acts of righteousness" versus "difficult acts of righteousness"?

The indication of the verses — with the constraints they contain — of this is at the highest level of clarity and manifestness. Striving was constrained therein by faith in Allah and the Last Day, while providing water and maintenance were left absolute without being constrained by faith. Then Allah, the Exalted, said: «*لا يَسْتَوُونَ عِنْدَ اللهِ*» (They are not equal with Allah), and then added: «*وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ*» (and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people).

Far be it that the one who performs acts of righteousness with Allah should be among the "wrongdoing people" who are deprived of the blessing of divine guidance.

Even if we assume that what is intended by "the wrongdoers" are those believers who were equating or preferring providing water and maintenance over striving, a believer, despite his faith, if he judges with such a judgment, is merely one who has erred and will be guided if shown the correct way, not a "wrongdoer" deprived of guidance. Understand this.

Third: What has preceded, that His saying: «*كَمَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ*» (like [the person] who has faith...) and His saying: «*لا يَسْتَوُونَ*» (They are not equal...) is evidence that the person (الشخص) has a role in the judgment contained in the verses.

Contemplation of the noble verses and reflection on what we have mentioned here and there clarifies to the critical researcher that the weakest of the narrations and the furthest from being applicable to the content of the verses is the narration of al-Nu'man ibn Bashir, for it does not accept application to the noble verses with the constraints they contain.

Following it in weakness is the narration of Ibn Sirin and those narrations in its meaning, for their apparent sense is that al-Abbas was invited to migrate while he was a Muslim, so he boasted of providing water and gatekeeping, and the verses do not support that, as has passed.

Furthermore, what occurs in the narration of Ibn Sirin is al-Abbas's mention of providing water and the gatekeeping of the House, yet he did not have the gatekeeping; it was only the *siqayah*.

Following it in weakness is the narration of Ibn Abbas, for its apparent sense is that the comparison was only between the deeds themselves, and the verse does not support that.

Furthermore, it contains that al-Abbas mentioned, among what he mentioned, providing water to the pilgrims, maintaining the Mosque, and ransoming the captive (فك العاني). If it were so, it would have been mentioned in the verse. This has also occurred in the narration of Ibn Jarir and Abu al-Shaykh from al-Dahhak regarding this meaning, who said: The Muslims turned to al-Abbas and his companions—those who were captured on the day of Badr—reproaching them for polytheism. Al-Abbas said: "By Allah, we used to maintain the Sacred Mosque, ransom the captive, gatekeep the House, and provide water to the pilgrims." Then Allah revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِ*» the verse. The discussion regarding ransoming the captive and gatekeeping the House occurring in it is like the discussion regarding what preceded it.

Thus, the soundest of the narrations in this chapter and the closest to being applicable to the verses in content is the narration of al-Qurazi and what is in its meaning, such as the narration of al-Hakim in *al-Mustadrak*, the narration of Abd al-Razzaq from al-Hasan, and the narration of Abu Nu'aym and Ibn Asakir from Anas that follows. The clarification of that has already preceded.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu Nu'aym narrated in *Fada'il al-Sahabah* and Ibn Asakir from Anas, who said: Al-Abbas and Shaybah, the master of the House, sat boasting to one another. Al-Abbas said: "I am more noble than you; I am the uncle of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the executor (وصي) of his father, and the provider of water to the pilgrims." Shaybah said: "I am more noble than you; I am the trustee of Allah over His House and its treasurer. Why did He not entrust you as He entrusted me?"

Ali came upon them, and they informed him of what they had said. Ali said: "I am more noble than you both; I am the first to believe and migrate." The three of them went to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him, but he did not answer them with anything. They departed, and the revelation descended upon him after some days. He sent for them and recited to them: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِّ وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ*» until the end of the ten verses.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, from his father, from Safwan, from Ibn Muskan, from Abu Basir, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "It was revealed concerning Ali, al-Abbas, and Shaybah. Al-Abbas said: 'I am superior because the providing of water to the pilgrims is in my hand.' Shaybah said: 'I am superior because the gatekeeping of the House is in my hand.' Ali said: 'I am superior, for I believed before you both, then I migrated and strove.' They sought the judgment of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and Allah revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِ*» until His saying: «*إِنَّ اللهَ عِنْدَهُ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ*»."

**I say:** It was also narrated by al-Ayyashi from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) in a similar fashion, and it mentions Uthman ibn Abi Shaybah in place of Shaybah.

In *al-Kafi*, from Abu Ali al-Ash'ari, from Muhammad ibn Abd al-Jabbar, from Safwan ibn Yahya, from Ibn Muskan, from Abu Basir, from one of the two Imams (peace be upon them): Regarding the saying of Allah: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِّ وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ ـ كَمَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ*», it was revealed concerning Hamzah, Ali, Ja'far, al-Abbas, and Shaybah. They boasted of providing water and gatekeeping, so Allah, mighty is His mention, revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِّ وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ ـ كَمَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ*». Ali, Hamzah, and Ja'far were those who believed in Allah and the Last Day and strove in the way of Allah. They are not equal with Allah.

**I say:** It was also narrated by al-Ayyashi in his commentary from Abu Basir from one of the two Imams (peace be upon them) in a similar fashion.

The narration does not harmonize with what definitive transmission establishes. Hamzah was among the first Emigrants who joined the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, then he was martyred in the Battle of Uhud in the third year of the Hijrah. Ja'far had migrated to Abyssinia before the Prophet's migration, then returned to Medina during the days of the Conquest of Khaybar, and Hamzah had been martyred a period before that. If the five of them had gathered to boast, it would have been before the Prophetic migration, and in that case, what is the meaning of what occurs in the narration: "Ali, Hamzah, and Ja'far were those who believed in Allah and the Last Day and strove in the way of Allah"?

If what is intended by its being "revealed concerning them" is the application (الجري) of the verse to them, then al-Abbas was like them, for he believed on the day he was captured at Badr and then attended some of the Prophet's battles.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from *al-Jam' bayn al-Sihah al-Sittah* by al-Abdi, in the second part of *Sahih al-Nasa'i*, with his chain of transmission, he said: Talhah ibn Shaybah from the tribe of Abd al-Dar, al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, and Ali ibn Abi Talib boasted to one another. Talhah said: "In my hand is the key to the House; if I wished, I could spend the night in it." Al-Abbas said: "I am the master of the *siqayah* and its overseer; if I wished, I could spend the night in the Mosque." Ali said: "I do not know what you two are saying; I prayed toward the Qiblah six months before the people, and I am the master of jihad." Then Allah revealed: «*أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِّ وَعِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ*» the verse.

**I say:** What is intended by praying six months before the people is precedence in faith in Allah, as the verse addresses; otherwise, it would have been necessary for it to be mentioned in the verse. The third of the group was mentioned as Talhah ibn Shaybah, and it has preceded in some narrations that it was Shaybah, and in others that it was Uthman ibn Abi Shaybah.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Shahrashub, from Abu Hamzah, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): Regarding His saying, the Exalted: «*يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ـ لا تَتَّخِذُوا آباءَكُمْ وَإِخْوانَكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ ـ إِنِ اسْتَحَبُّوا الْكُفْرَ عَلَى الْإِيمانِ*» (O you who have faith! Do not befriend your fathers and brothers if they prefer unfaith to faith), he said: "Faith (الإيمان) is the guardianship (ولاية) of Ali ibn Abi Talib."

**I say:** This is from the inner meaning (باطن) of the Quran, based on the analysis of the meaning of faith into its levels of perfection.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: When the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) announced that no polytheist should enter the Sacred Mosque after that, the Quraysh were deeply distressed and said: "Our trade is gone, our families are lost, and our houses are ruined." Then Allah revealed regarding that: «*قُلْ*» — Say, O Muhammad — «*إِنْ كانَ آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ وَإِخْوانُكُمْ وَأَزْواجُكُمْ وَعَشِيرَتُكُمْ*» until His saying: «*وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ*».

**I say:** Based on this, it would be appropriate to interpret His saying in the verse: **حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ** "until Allah brings about His command" as a remedy for the economic stagnation (الكساد) that would befall them, and the opening of a door of provision (الرزق) for them from another direction. This is similar to what occurred in the saying of the Exalted within the following verses: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلَا يَقْرَبُوا الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرَامَ بَعْدَ عَامِهِمْ هَٰذَا ۚ وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ عَيْلَةً فَسَوْفَ يُغْنِيكُمُ اللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِ إِن شَاءَ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ** "O you who have faith! The polytheists are indeed impure. So let them not approach the Holy Mosque after this year of theirs. And if you fear poverty, Allah will enrich you out of His grace, if He wishes. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise." (9:28).

In fact, the context of the two verses would then be identical. However, the tone of gentleness and the nobility of address in a phrase like **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "O you who have faith" refuses to allow the address in His saying: **قُلْ إِن كَانَ آبَاؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَاؤُكُمْ** "Say, ‘If your fathers and your sons..." to be directed at those same individuals, given the harshness at the end of the verse in His saying: **وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفَاسِقِينَ** "and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot."

Furthermore, the verse mentions the love of fathers, brothers, kin, and the wealth they have acquired (اقترفوها), yet none of these were mentioned in the narration (الرواية). Nor was the loss of trade (ضيعة) considered in relation to them. What, then, would be the meaning of mentioning them in the verse and the threat (التهديد) regarding choosing the love of these things over the love of Allah and His Messenger? And what would be the meaning of mentioning struggle in His way (الجهاد في سبيله) in the verse? Reflect on that.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad and al-Bukhari narrated from ‘Abdullah ibn Hisham, who said: "We were with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while he was holding the hand of ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab. ‘Umar said: 'By Allah, O Messenger of Allah, you are indeed more beloved to me than everything except my own self.' The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: 'None of you truly believes until I am more beloved to him than his own self.'"
